title: Should Wizard Hit Mommy? by John Updike
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Parenting, generation gap, children’s moral judgments, adult authority, and how stories shape a child’s view of right and wrong.

summary:
The story is about Jack, a father who tells his four-year-old daughter Jo a bedtime story every evening. He makes up stories about a small creature named Roger, usually Roger Skunk, Roger Fish, or Roger Squirrel. The stories always follow a pattern: Roger has a problem, he goes to the wise old owl, the owl sends him to the wizard, and the wizard solves the problem. Then Roger happily goes home to his mommy.

This time, Jack tells the story of Roger Skunk, who smells so bad that no other animals play with him. He goes to the wizard, who makes him smell like roses. The other animals now play with him, and he is happy. But when Roger goes home, his mother is angry. She says she likes his original smell and hates the rose smell. She takes Roger back to the wizard and hits the wizard on the head, making him change Roger back to his original smell.

Jo is upset by this ending. She thinks the mother was wrong to hit the wizard and that Roger should keep smelling like roses so he can have friends. She wants her father to change the story so that the wizard hits Mommy. Jack refuses. He defends the mother, saying parents know what is best for their children. Jo insists that the wizard should hit the stupid mommy.

The story ends with Jack feeling troubled. He realizes that Jo is growing up and starting to question adult authority. He also feels guilty because he himself feels trapped in his marriage to Jo’s mother, Clare. The story forces him to confront his own feelings.
